parts.content: Explain how the structure of a phospholipid allows it to form biological membranes.
parts.markscheme:
- Phospholipids have a hydrophilic (polar) head <marks>[1]</marks>
- Phospholipids have hydrophobic (non-polar) tails <marks>[1]</marks>
- In water, phospholipids spontaneously form a bilayer <marks>[1]</marks>
- Hydrophobic tails face inward and hydrophilic heads face outward toward the aqueous environment <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Identify the amphipathic nature
steps.content:
To understand how phospholipids form membranes, we first look at their chemical nature. Phospholipids are **amphipathic**, meaning they have two distinct regions with opposite properties: a **hydrophilic** (water-loving) part and a **hydrophobic** (water-fearing) part.

This concept is a prerequisite covered in **Topic B1.1: Carbohydrates and lipids**, specifically regarding the hydrophobic properties of lipids.

steps.title: Describe the hydrophilic head
steps.content: The "head" of the phospholipid molecule contains a phosphate group. Because this group is polar/charged, it is **hydrophilic**. This allows the head to form hydrogen bonds with water molecules ($H_2O$).

steps.title: Describe the hydrophobic tails
steps.content: Attached to the head are two long hydrocarbon chains known as fatty acid **tails**. These chains are non-polar and **hydrophobic**, meaning they do not dissolve in water and instead repel it.

steps.title: Explain spontaneous bilayer formation
steps.content: When phospholipids are placed in an aqueous (water-based) environment, they **spontaneously** arrange themselves to find the most stable energetic state. To minimize the contact between the hydrophobic tails and water, the molecules organize into a **double layer**, or **bilayer**.

highlights.text: In water, phospholipids spontaneously form a bilayer

steps.title: Analyze the bilayer orientation
steps.content:
In this bilayer structure:

*   The **hydrophilic heads** face **outward**, interacting with the water inside the cell (cytoplasm) and outside the cell (extracellular fluid).
*   The **hydrophobic tails** face **inward**, pointing toward each other and staying away from the water.

This arrangement creates a stable barrier that forms the basis of all biological membranes.

highlights.text: Hydrophobic tails face inward and hydrophilic heads face outward toward the aqueous environment

steps.title: Define the phospholipid components
steps.content:
To understand biological membranes, we first look at the unique structure of a single phospholipid molecule. It consists of two distinct regions:
1. A **hydrophilic** (polar) head containing a phosphate group.
2. Two **hydrophobic** (non-polar) fatty acid tails.

You can review these building blocks in **Topic B1.1: Carbohydrates and lipids**.

steps.title: Explain the amphipathic nature
steps.content: Because the molecule has both a "water-loving" part and a "water-fearing" part, it is described as **amphipathic**. This chemical property is the driving force behind how membranes form. Since the environment inside and outside of cells is primarily water, the phospholipid must find a way to satisfy both regions of its structure simultaneously.

steps.title: Describe spontaneous self-assembly
steps.content: When phospholipids are placed in an aqueous (water-based) environment, they **spontaneously** organize themselves without needing any energy input. They form a **bilayer**—a double layer of molecules—which is the most energetically stable configuration for amphipathic molecules in water.

highlights.text: In water, phospholipids spontaneously form a bilayer

steps.title: Detail the bilayer orientation
steps.content:
The bilayer forms a specific orientation to manage its interaction with water:
- The **hydrophilic heads face outward**, interacting with the water in the cytoplasm and the extracellular fluid.
- The **hydrophobic tails face inward**, pointing toward each other in the center of the membrane.

This arrangement effectively **shields the tails from water**, creating a stable, semi-permeable barrier that serves as the basis for all biological membranes.

highlights.text: Hydrophobic tails face inward and hydrophilic heads face outward toward the aqueous environment

parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Explain why species in cold environments have a higher percentage of unsaturated fats compared to species in hot environments.
parts.markscheme:
- Unsaturated fats contain one or more double bonds that create kinks in hydrocarbon chains, preventing close packing and lowering melting point compared to saturated fats. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A higher proportion of unsaturated fats helps lipids remain fluid at low temperatures (reduced solidification), maintaining biological function (e.g., membrane fluidity). <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points:
- Less tight packing reduces intermolecular (van der Waals) forces, contributing to a lower melting point. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Saturated fats pack tightly and are more likely to be solid at low temperatures, so they are less suitable in cold environments. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the structure of cellulose relates to its function in plant cell walls.
parts.markscheme:
- Cellulose is a polymer of $\beta$-glucose; <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Linked by $\beta$-1,4 glycosidic bonds; <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Glucose subunits are alternately rotated $180^\circ$; <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- This forms long, straight, unbranched chains; <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Hydrogen bonds form between chains; <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Chains form bundles called microfibrils; <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Microfibrils provide high tensile strength; <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Allows resistance to turgor pressure; <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Maintains cell shape and supports plant structure; <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the molecular arrangement of the phospholipid bilayer contributes to selective permeability.
parts.markscheme:
- Hydrophobic fatty acid tails face inward creating a non-polar core that restricts polar/charged substances but allows small non-polar molecules to diffuse <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hydrophilic phosphate heads face the aqueous environments, giving an amphipathic barrier that selectively permits passage based on polarity/charge (and size) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Small polar molecules such as water can pass to some extent (often via channels in cells) compared with larger polar molecules <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.content: Triglycerides have a lower energy density, allowing for a more controlled release of energy during flight.
Triglycerides are more soluble in the bloodstream, enabling faster transport to active wing muscles.
The lack of associated water and higher energy density significantly reduce the total body mass to be carried.
options.markscheme: Lipids (triglycerides) are much more efficient for energy storage in mobile animals because they are hydrophobic and stored without water of hydration. As shown in the table, glycogen associates with $$2.0\,g$$ of water for every gram of carbohydrate, making it over six times heavier than an equivalent energy amount stored as lipid ($$17\,kJ$$ per $$3.0\,g$$ of wet mass vs $$38\,kJ$$ per $$1.0\,g$$ of lipid mass).

options.content: Glycogen is more hydrophobic than triglycerides, which prevents it from interfering with cellular osmosis.
